= Software Waveform Analysis in prad2dec =

''Source: [https://github.com/Chao1009/prad2evviewer/blob/main/docs/prad2dec/wave_analysis.md docs/prad2dec/wave_analysis.md] in the prad2evviewer repo.''

<code>prad2dec</code> ships '''two''' offline analyzers that run on raw FADC250 samples (<code>uint16_t[nsamples]</code>, 4 ns/sample at 250 MHz):

{| class="wikitable"
! Analyzer
! Class
! Purpose
|-
| Waveform
| <code>fdec::WaveAnalyzer</code> (<code>WaveAnalyzer.{h,cpp}</code>)
| Robust local-maxima peak finding for HyCal energy / time use. Tolerates noisy pedestals, finds multiple peaks per channel.
|-
| Firmware emulator
| <code>fdec::Fadc250FwAnalyzer</code> (<code>Fadc250FwAnalyzer.{h,cpp}</code>)
| Bit-faithful emulation of the JLab FADC250 firmware Mode 1/2/3 (Hall-D V3 + NSAT/NPED/MAXPED extensions). Used to compare offline reconstruction against on-board firmware output.
|}

Both are stack-allocated, zero-heap on the hot path, and run side-by-side when <code>prad2ana_replay_rawdata</code> is invoked with the <code>-p</code> flag (see [[REPLAYED_DATA]]).

The remainder of this note walks through both algorithms on a single example pulse, with parameter values matching the real run config in <code>database/daq_config.json</code>.

== Example waveform ==

100 samples × 4 ns. A short, bright pulse on top of a quiet ~146 ADC baseline, followed by a long scintillation tail.

[[File:WA_fig1_overview.png|center|900px|Example waveform overview]]

{| class="wikitable"
! feature
! value
|-
| length
| 100 samples = 400 ns
|-
| baseline
| ≈ 146 ADC (samples 0..29)
|-
| pulse onset
| sample 30 (t = 120 ns)
|-
| peak
| sample 32, 1393 ADC
|-
| rise time
| 8 ns (cross → peak)
|-
| tail
| slow exponential decay, still ~20 ADC above baseline at sample 99
|}

This is representative of a HyCal PbWO<sub>4</sub> signal: a fast leading edge (~10 ns) followed by a long PMT/scintillator tail.

== Waveform analyzer — WaveAnalyzer ==

Used for HyCal calibration / monitoring where we want a robust peak height and a generous integral that follows the actual pulse shape rather than a fixed firmware window.

=== Parameters ===

All settings live in <code>fdec::WaveConfig</code> (see <code>WaveAnalyzer.h</code>); the values shown are the defaults.

{| class="wikitable"
! field
! default
! unit
! role
|-
| <code>resolution</code>
| 2
| half-width
| Triangular smoothing kernel half-width. <code>1</code> disables smoothing. Larger values smear the rising edge but suppress per-sample noise.
|-
| <code>threshold</code>
| 5.0
| × pedestal RMS
| Peak-acceptance threshold above the local baseline.
|-
| <code>min_threshold</code>
| 3.0
| ADC counts
| Hard floor on the acceptance threshold — protects quiet channels where <code>5·rms</code> would underflow.
|-
| <code>min_peak_ratio</code>
| 0.3
| fraction
| When two peaks overlap, the secondary must be ≥ this fraction of the primary's height to survive.
|-
| <code>int_tail_ratio</code>
| 0.1
| fraction
| Integration stops when the pedsub waveform drops below <code>r × peak height</code>. Smaller values capture more of the tail.
|-
| <code>ped_nsamples</code>
| 30
| samples
| Window used for the pedestal estimate (start of the buffer).
|-
| <code>ped_flatness</code>
| 1.0
| ADC counts
| Floor on the outlier-rejection band: samples are kept iff <code><nowiki>|s − μ| < max(rms, ped_flatness)</nowiki></code>. Prevents over-tight clipping on already-quiet baselines.
|-
| <code>ped_max_iter</code>
| 3
| iterations
| Maximum outlier-rejection passes. Stops early if the mask doesn't change or fewer than 5 samples remain.
|-
| <code>overflow</code>
| 4095
| ADC counts
| 12-bit overflow value — peaks at this height are tagged.
|-
| <code>clk_mhz</code>
| 250.0
| MHz
| Sample rate for the time conversion <code>t_ns = pos · 1000 / clk_mhz</code>.
|}

=== Pipeline ===

'''1. Triangular smoothing.''' <code>resolution = 2</code> (default) → <code>buf[i] = (raw[i−1]·w + raw[i] + raw[i+1]·w) / (1 + 2w)</code> with <code>w = 1 − 1/(res+1)</code>. With <code>res = 1</code> smoothing is disabled.

'''2. Iterative pedestal.''' First <code>ped_nsamples = 30</code> samples of the ''smoothed'' trace. Iterate up to <code>ped_max_iter = 3</code> times:

* compute <code>mean</code>, <code>rms</code>
* drop samples deviating more than <code>max(rms, ped_flatness)</code> from the mean
* re-compute

For our trace: <code>mean = 145.61</code>, <code>rms = 0.45</code> after convergence.

'''3. Threshold.''' <code>thr = max(threshold·rms, min_threshold) = max(5·0.45, 3.0) = 3.0</code>. The hard floor <code>min_threshold</code> keeps the threshold sane on quiet channels.

'''4. Local-maxima search.''' Walk smoothed buffer; a peak is accepted iff:

* it is a local max (with flat-plateau handling)
* its height above the '''local baseline''' (linear interpolation between the surrounding minima) exceeds <code>thr</code>
* its height above the '''pedestal mean''' exceeds both <code>thr</code> and <code>3·rms</code>

'''5. Integration.''' Walk outward from the peak, summing pedsub values until <code>s′ < tail_cut = int_tail_ratio · ped_height</code> (default 10 % of peak height) or <code>s′ < ped_rms</code>. This adapts to the pulse shape — wide pulses get wide windows, narrow pulses get narrow ones.

'''6. Raw-position correction.''' The recorded <code>pos</code> is the raw-sample maximum near the smoothed peak (not the smoothed peak itself), so the reported height equals the actual ADC at the peak rather than a smoothed under-estimate.

For our trace:

{| class="wikitable"
! field
! value
|-
| <code>peak.pos</code>
| sample 32 (t = 128.0 ns)
|-
| <code>peak.height</code>
| 1247 ADC (raw − pedestal)
|-
| <code>peak.left, peak.right</code>
| 28, 49 (integration bounds)
|-
| <code>peak.integral</code>
| 9600 (ADC·sample, pedsub)
|}

[[File:WA_fig3_soft_analysis.png|center|900px|Waveform analyzer — smoothing, pedestal, peak, tail-cutoff integration]]

=== Parameter sensitivity ===

Two of the parameters above visibly change the analyzer's output on this trace:

[[File:WA_fig4_soft_parameters.png|center|1100px|Pedestal iterative outlier rejection (left) and int_tail_ratio sensitivity (right)]]

'''Left — pedestal <code>ped_flatness</code> × <code>ped_max_iter</code>.''' All 30 baseline samples enter pass 1; samples deviating from the running mean by more than <code>max(rms, ped_flatness) = 1.0</code> are dropped, the mean and rms are recomputed, and the procedure repeats. After convergence the band has collapsed onto the dominant cluster (146 / 147 ADC) and the outliers (values 143 / 144 / 145 / 150) are off the kept set. <code>ped_flatness</code> sets the noise floor below which the band stops shrinking — without it, a particularly quiet channel could iterate down to a band of zero width and reject everything except the modal value.

<blockquote>
'''Note:''' the demo runs the procedure on the ''raw'' samples for readability (integer values plot cleanly); the C++ runs it on the smoothed buffer, which is why the converged mean shown here (146.50) is slightly higher than the analyzer's reported value (145.61). The kept/rejected pattern is the same in both cases.
</blockquote>

'''Right — <code>int_tail_ratio</code>.''' The integration walks outward from the peak and stops when the pedsub waveform falls below <code>r × peak height</code>. For this slow tail:

{| class="wikitable"
! <code>int_tail_ratio</code>
! window
! samples
! integral
|-
| 0.20
| [30, 41]
| 12
| 8376
|-
| 0.10 (default)
| [30, 47]
| 18
| 9477
|-
| 0.05
| [30, 57]
| 28
| 10332
|}

The default of 0.10 is a good compromise: it captures the prompt peak plus the first ~70 ns of the tail, missing only the slow scintillation component (which is also where pile-up from the next event lives). Smaller <code>r</code> recovers more tail energy but increases sensitivity to baseline drift and downstream pulses.

'''Smoothing — <code>resolution</code>.''' On the bright pulse above, smoothing is invisible at the scale of a 1247 ADC peak. It earns its keep on small-signal channels where the per-sample fluctuation is comparable to the pulse height. The figure below uses a different waveform — a small ~24 ADC bump on a baseline with ±3 ADC zig-zag — to show what the kernel does:

[[File:WA_fig5_smoothing.png|center|1100px|Smoothing on a low-S/N pulse — raw vs res=2 vs res=4]]

{| class="wikitable"
! <code>resolution</code>
! spurious local maxima above +2 ADC
! peak height (smoothed)
|-
| 1 (raw)
| 6
| 169
|-
| 2 (default)
| 3
| 166
|-
| 4
| 1
| 162
|}

<code>res = 1</code> keeps the raw zig-zag — six local maxima clear +2 ADC, the peak finder needs every other rejection rule (height-above-baseline, 3·rms, peak-overlap ratio) to find the real one. <code>res = 2</code> collapses the zig-zag without visibly attenuating the pulse. <code>res = 4</code> removes essentially all baseline structure but starts to clip the peak by ~7 ADC, so its use should be reserved for very low-S/N channels where peak-finding robustness is worth more than peak-height fidelity.

The remaining parameters affect the bright-pulse example only marginally:

* <code>threshold = 5</code> × the post-clipping rms (0.45) gives 2.25 ADC, below the floor; <code>min_threshold = 3</code> wins. For a noisier channel with rms ≳ 1 ADC the <code>5·rms</code> rule kicks in.
* <code>min_peak_ratio</code> only matters when two peaks share an integration range — single-pulse waveforms never trigger it.

== Firmware emulator — Fadc250FwAnalyzer ==

The firmware analyzer reproduces the on-board pipeline so we can compare offline analysis against firmware-reported values without re-running the DAQ. The full algorithm spec lives in <code>docs/clas_fadc/FADC250_algorithms.md</code>; this section is a parameter-by-parameter walk-through.

=== Parameters ===

Parameters live under the <code>fadc250_firmware</code> block in <code>daq_config.json</code>. '''<code>NSB</code> and <code>NSA</code> are in nanoseconds''', floored to whole 4 ns samples inside the analyzer; everything else is unitless or in ADC counts.

{| class="wikitable"
! field
! unit
! role
|-
| <code>TET</code>
| ADC counts
| Trigger Energy Threshold above pedestal. A pulse is rejected if <code>Vpeak − Vmin ≤ TET</code>.
|-
| <code>NSB</code>
| ns
| Window before threshold crossing (Mode 2 integral). Floored to whole samples (<code>NSB / CLK_NS</code>).
|-
| <code>NSA</code>
| ns
| Window after threshold crossing. Same flooring as <code>NSB</code>.
|-
| <code>NPEAK</code> (= <code>MAX_PULSES</code>)
| —
| Max pulses kept per channel per readout window (1..4).
|-
| <code>NSAT</code>
| samples
| Consecutive-above-TET requirement after Tcross — rejects single-sample spikes. <code>NSAT=1</code> reproduces the legacy Mode 3 single-sample crossing.
|-
| <code>NPED</code>
| samples
| Number of leading samples summed for the <code>Vnoise</code> estimate.
|-
| <code>MAXPED</code>
| ADC counts
| Online outlier-rejection threshold: pedsub samples whose deviation exceeds <code>MAXPED</code> are dropped from the <code>Vnoise</code> sum. <code>0</code> disables.
|-
| <code>CLK_NS</code>
| ns
| Sample period (4 ns at 250 MHz).
|}

Run-config defaults (current <code>daq_config.json</code>):

<syntaxhighlight lang="json">
"TET": 10.0, "NSB": 8, "NSA": 128, "NPEAK": 1,
"NSAT": 4, "NPED": 3, "MAXPED": 1, "CLK_NS": 4.0
</syntaxhighlight>

=== Pipeline ===

Step-by-step, applied to the example waveform:

'''1. Pedestal estimate (<code>Vnoise</code>).''' Mean of the first <code>NPED = 3</code> samples with <code>MAXPED = 1</code> outlier filter (drop any sample whose deviation from the running mean exceeds 1 ADC). For our trace: <code>(146+147+144)/3 = 145.67</code>, sample 1 (147) is filtered, refined mean = <code>145.0</code>.

'''2. Pulse search.''' <code>Vmin = Vnoise</code>. Walk the buffer starting at sample <code>NPED</code>. The first pulse is detected as soon as a sample exceeds <code>Vnoise</code> and walks monotonically up to a local maximum.

'''3. Acceptance.''' <code>Vpeak = 1393</code>. The pedestal-subtracted height is <code>Vpeak − Vmin = 1247 ≫ TET = 10</code> → accepted.

'''4. Tcross.''' First leading-edge sample whose pedsub value exceeds <code>TET</code>: sample 30, since <code>637 − 146 = 491 > 10</code>.

'''5. NSAT gate.''' <code>NSAT = 4</code> → samples 30, 31, 32, 33 must all be > TET. They are (491, 1221, 1247, 1093) → accepted. With <code>NSAT = 1</code>, this gate is a no-op.

'''6. TDC — <code>Va</code>, bracket, fine time.'''

<pre>
Va  = Vmin + (Vpeak − Vmin) / 2
    = 146 + (1393 − 146) / 2
    = 769.5
</pre>

Find the bracket on the rising edge: smallest <code>k</code> with <code>s[k] ≥ Va</code>. Here <code>s[30] = 637 < 769.5</code>, <code>s[31] = 1367 ≥ 769.5</code> → <code>k = 31</code>. So <code>Vba = s[30] = 637</code>, <code>Vaa = s[31] = 1367</code>. Fine time:

<pre>
fine = round( (Va − Vba) / (Vaa − Vba) × 64 )
     = round( (769.5 − 637) / (1367 − 637) × 64 )
     = round( 0.1815 × 64 ) = 12

coarse     = k − 1 = 30
time_units = coarse·64 + fine = 1932    (LSB = 62.5 ps)
time_ns    = time_units × CLK_NS / 64 = 120.75 ns
</pre>

Visually (left panel of the figure below): the dot-dash <code>Va</code> line crosses the rising edge between the diamond <code>Vba</code> and the square <code>Vaa</code> markers. The fine-time arrow points from the <code>Vba</code> sample to the interpolated zero-crossing.

'''7. Mode-2 integral.''' Window <code>[cross − NSB_s, cross + NSA_s]</code> where <code>NSB_s = NSB/4 = 2</code>, <code>NSA_s = NSA/4 = 32</code>. So <code>[28, 62]</code>, i.e. 35 samples = 140 ns. The integrand is the pedestal-subtracted waveform <code>s′ = max(0, s − Vnoise)</code>.

<pre>
Σ s′[28..62] = 10589  (pedsub ADC·sample)
</pre>

The shaded band in the right panel is exactly this sum.

[[File:WA_fig2_firmware_analysis.png|center|1100px|Firmware TDC bracket (left) and Mode-2 integration window with NSB/NSA (right)]]

'''8. Quality bitmask.''' <code>0x00</code> = <code>Q_DAQ_GOOD</code>. Set bits would indicate:

{| class="wikitable"
! bit
! flag
! condition
|-
| <code>1 << 0</code>
| <code>Q_DAQ_PEAK_AT_BOUNDARY</code>
| peak landed on the last sample
|-
| <code>1 << 1</code>
| <code>Q_DAQ_NSB_TRUNCATED</code>
| <code>cross − NSB_s < 0</code>, window clipped
|-
| <code>1 << 2</code>
| <code>Q_DAQ_NSA_TRUNCATED</code>
| <code>cross + NSA_s ≥ N</code>, window clipped
|-
| <code>1 << 3</code>
| <code>Q_DAQ_VA_OUT_OF_RANGE</code>
| <code>Va</code> not bracketed on the rising edge (numerical edge case)
|}

== Side-by-side comparison ==

{| class="wikitable"
! field
! waveform (<code>WaveAnalyzer</code>)
! firmware (<code>Fadc250FwAnalyzer</code>)
|-
| pedestal
| 145.61 ± 0.45 (30 samples, σ-clip)
| 145.0 (3 samples, MAXPED filter)
|-
| time
| 128.0 ns (raw peak sample × 4)
| 120.75 ns (TDC mid-amplitude interp.)
|-
| height
| 1247 ADC (raw − ped)
| 1247 ADC (<code>Vpeak − Vmin</code>)
|-
| integral window
| [28, 49] (22 samples, tail-driven)
| [28, 62] (35 samples, fixed NSB/NSA)
|-
| integral
| 9600
| 10589
|}

The waveform analyzer's ''time'' is the peak sample (rounded to 4 ns); the firmware's ''time'' is mid-amplitude on the rising edge with 62.5 ps LSB — they are intentionally different observables.

The firmware's wider window (140 ns vs the waveform analyzer's 88 ns) picks up more of the slow scintillation tail. With <code>NSA = 128 ns</code> the window stops at sample 62; the rest of the tail (samples 63..99) is excluded.

== Reproducing the plots ==

Both algorithms are re-implemented in <code>plot_wave_analysis.py</code> (NumPy + Matplotlib only).

<syntaxhighlight lang="bash">
cd docs/prad2dec
python plot_wave_analysis.py
</syntaxhighlight>

Regenerates <code>figs/fig1_overview.png</code>, <code>figs/fig2_firmware_analysis.png</code>, <code>figs/fig3_soft_analysis.png</code>, <code>figs/fig4_soft_parameters.png</code>, <code>figs/fig5_smoothing.png</code> and prints the numeric results above.

== See also ==

* <code>docs/clas_fadc/FADC250_algorithms.md</code> — full firmware algorithm spec with manual cross-references
* <code>prad2dec/include/WaveAnalyzer.h</code>, <code>prad2dec/src/WaveAnalyzer.cpp</code> — C++ source
* <code>prad2dec/include/Fadc250FwAnalyzer.h</code>, <code>prad2dec/src/Fadc250FwAnalyzer.cpp</code> — C++ source
* <code>docs/REPLAYED_DATA.md</code> — branch layout for the replay tree (where both analyzer outputs land)
